title: Parametrised Whitehead Torsion of Families of Nearby Lagrangians
speaker: Noah Porcelli
abstract:
The parametrised Whitehead torsion is an invariant of families of manifolds, and can be viewed as a map to an algebraic K-theory space. A strong version of the nearby Lagrangian conjecture says that when applied to families of closed exact Lagrangians in a cotangent bundle, this invariant vanishes.
Abouzaid and Kragh showed that in this case, this map lands in the trivial path component of the target, i.e. is trivial on π0
. Using generating functions, we find strong constraints on what this map does to higher homotopy groups. I'll illustrate this with some concrete consequences for the symplectic mapping class group of T∗Tn
 relative to the 0-section.
This is based on joint work-in-progress with Sylvain Courte.

proof_stages.label: Nearby and strong nearby Lagrangian setting
proof_stages.summary: The talk starts from the nearby Lagrangian conjecture for closed exact Lagrangians L in T^*Q, recalls known homotopy and simple-homotopy information, and upgrades the object of study to the full space of nearby Lagrangians with its tautological fiber bundle.

proof_stages.label: Stable h-cobordism target
proof_stages.summary: The target is explained by recalling h-cobordisms on Q, the h-cobordism space H(Q) whose homotopy groups are represented by families over spheres, its stabilized colimit, and Waldhausen-style algebraic K-theoretic computations of its homotopy groups.

proof_stages.label: Parametrized Whitehead torsion invariant
proof_stages.summary: A family invariant W from the space of nearby Lagrangians to the stable h-cobordism space of Q is announced and then geometrically constructed from complements of tubular neighborhoods, with its triviality interpreted as stable diffeomorphic equivalence between L and Q.

proof_stages.label: Consequences and computable restrictions
proof_stages.summary: The theorem is translated into consequences: pi_0 recovers the Abouzaid-Kragh simple-homotopy equivalence result, pi_1 gives restrictions on loops of nearby Lagrangians, and concrete mapping-class information can be extracted for high-dimensional tori.

proof_stages.label: Factorization through the point and Euler characteristic
proof_stages.summary: The refined statement factors W through a map delta to the stable h-cobordism space of a point and then through product with Q, with a further Euler-characteristic factorization explaining stronger vanishing when chi(Q)=0.

proof_stages.label: Generating-function and difference-function input
proof_stages.summary: Abouzaid-Kragh twisted generating functions for nearby Lagrangians are replaced by an untwisted difference function F(x,v_1,v_2)=f(x,v_1)-f(x,v_2), which is quadratic at infinity and has Morse-Bott critical set diffeomorphic to L.

proof_stages.label: Gradient-flow construction of delta
proof_stages.summary: Using the local quadratic and Morse-Bott gradient-flow models near infinity and near L, the negative-gradient flow matches negative boundary pieces so that complements represent W and, after sphere-bundle homotopy manipulations, produce the h-cobordism-on-a-disk family defining delta(L).

examples.name: Known low-dimensional bases for the nearby Lagrangian conjecture
examples.note: The bases S^1, S^2, RP^2, and T^2 illustrate the scarcity of complete known cases and motivate passing to weaker invariants rather than supplying a new idea for the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: The low-dimensional cases S1, S2, RP2, and T2 are listed as known cases of the nearby Lagrangian conjecture without doing any computation or construction on them.

examples.name: Three-dimensional lens spaces
examples.note: Lens spaces are used to show that pi_0-vanishing of the torsion gives simple-homotopy information strong enough to imply diffeomorphism in cases not detected by ordinary homotopy equivalence.
examples.engagement_reason: Three-dimensional lens spaces are cited as a case where simple homotopy equivalence implies diffeomorphism, but no lens-space calculation is carried out.

passes.reason: Lens spaces are used to show that homotopy equivalence alone does not detect diffeomorphism type, whereas simple homotopy torsion can

Lens spaces are spherical cyclic quotients, used here to show that homotopy equivalence alone does not detect diffeomorphism type.

examples.name: High-dimensional torus mapping-class obstruction
examples.note: For Q=T^n with n at least 6, a loop of nearby Lagrangians yields a self-diffeomorphism of T^n, and the theorem is illustrated by the vanishing of its projection to the infinite Z/2-summand part of the computed mapping class group.
examples.engagement_reason: For a high-dimensional torus the speaker traces a loop of Lagrangians to a Hamiltonian diffeomorphism, restricts it to the zero section, compares its mapping class components, and applies the theorem to force the infinite component to vanish.

passes.reason: The high-dimensional torus case is used to obstruct loop-induced diffeomorphisms with nonzero infinite mapping-class component
the high-dimensional torus is a highly regular abelian manifold used to rule out nonzero infinite mapping-class components from such loops
The high-dimensional torus is a flat homogeneous manifold, and a nonzero exotic mapping-class component would obstruct isotopy to the identity.
The high-dimensional torus is a flat highly regular manifold, and the vanishing theorem obstructs nonzero components in part of its mapping class group.
The high-dimensional torus is a flat highly symmetric manifold, and the result obstructs loops from inducing nonzero infinite mapping-class components.
The high-dimensional torus is a flat homogeneous manifold, and its nonzero mapping-class components serve as obstructions to isotopy/realizability by such loops.
The high-dimensional torus case is used to rule out nonzero components in the mapping-class group for such Lagrangian loops, and the torus is a highly regular flat manifold.
The high-dimensional torus case gives an obstruction ruling out nonzero infinite mapping-class components from Lagrangian loops, and T^n is a highly symmetric flat manifold.
For the highly symmetric torus, the nonzero infinite mapping-class component is presented as something a Lagrangian loop cannot realize.
The high-dimensional torus case is presented as a potential mapping-class obstruction to loop triviality, while the torus itself is a highly regular product Lie-group manifold.
The high-dimensional torus case gives an obstruction showing certain mapping-class components cannot arise from such loops, with the torus a highly regular flat manifold.
the high-dimensional torus is a regular flat homogeneous test case for mapping-class constraints, not an actual failure example

examples.engagement_reason: The speaker uses the circle cobordism picture to check the definition, distinguishing the genus cobordism as not an h-cobordism and the trivial cylinder as one.
